feed.title: Combined summary - Compact Isogeny PQC can replace HD wallets, key-tweaking, silent payments

entry.title: Combined summary - Compact Isogeny PQC can replace HD wallets, key-tweaking, silent payments
entry.summary: The exploration of post-quantum cryptography has spotlighted isogeny-based cryptographic methods as a compact, promising alternative to traditional lattice-based schemes. These methods revolve around the mapping of points between different elliptic curves or on the same curve through functions known as isogenies and endomorphisms. Isogeny-based cryptography stands out for its space efficiency in key and signature sizes, typically requiring less than 300 bytes, offering an attractive option compared to the several kilobytes needed in lattice crypto schemes. The focus of research in this area aims to make isogeny crypto accessible to bitcoin engineers well-versed in classical Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC), with the goal of incorporating these advanced cryptographic techniques into practical applications such as signature schemes, unhardened BIP32 key derivation, silent payments, and taproot-style key tweaking. Isogenies are defined as functions that allow the mapping between points on different elliptic curves, while endomorphisms map points within the same curve. The computational challenges associated with finding an isogeny between two given elliptic curves and determining the endomorphism ring of a curve without explicit knowledge are significant hurdles in cryptography. However, certain supersingular elliptic curves have well-known endomorphism rings, which can be leveraged for cryptographic uses. The equivalence of the supersingular isogeny path problem (SIPP) to the endomorphism ring problem (ERP) suggests that computing isogenies between elliptic curves becomes feasible when their endomorphism rings are known, forming the foundation for secure cryptographic keys. This principle also enables efficient computation of new isogenies and endomorphism rings from existing ones, providing potential defenses against quantum-enabled attackers. Despite notable advancements, developing multisignature isogeny schemes that match the compactness of existing methods remains an ongoing challenge. Researchers encourage more bitcoin developers to engage in the evolution of isogeny-based cryptography, drawing from expertise in classical ECC multisignature schemes. Further insights and discussions on this topic can be found at [DelvingBitcoin.org](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/post-quantum-hd-wallets-silent-payments-key-aggregation-and-threshold-signatures/1854) and [Conduition.io](https://conduition.io/cryptography/isogenies-intro/). In the broader cryptographic landscape, zero-knowledge proofs of knowledge (zkpok) are explored through cryptographic protocols transitioning from elliptic curve cryptography to isogeny-based systems. The creation of zkpok hinges on one-way mappings endowed with a homomorphic property between groups, facilitating the binding of a secret to a challenge value. Although this process does not achieve true zero-knowledge due to inherent limitations, division due to the public nature of the challenge circumvents this issue. Pedersen commitments exemplify the feasibility of constructing commit-challenge-response protocols that yield signatures, illustrating the adaptability of these concepts to isogeny-based systems despite differences in operations compared to abelian groups. The discourse on adopting post-quantum cryptographic primitives emphasizes evaluating properties beyond functionality, including 'rerandomization,' 'aggregatability,' and 'batch verifiability,' crucial for future-proofing security protocols against industry adoption challenges. Furthermore, the correspondence elaborates on the complexities of isogeny-based cryptography (IBC), notably the role of base curves and the necessity of selecting specific isogenies as secret keys for computational feasibility and security. IBC diverges from classical ECC in its approach to composable maps or isogeny composition, highlighting the innovation and limitations within this domain. Different implementations, such as CSIDH and SIDH, offer unique challenges and vulnerabilities, emphasizing the need for continuous research in securing and enhancing IBC protocols. The discussion extends to cryptographic proofs, security assurances, and the exploration of desirable cryptographic properties critical for blockchain technologies. Advanced research efforts, such as those demonstrated by [SQIsignHD](https://eprint.iacr.org/2023/436), showcase the trade-offs and exploratory nature of current investigations into improving IBC's applicability and efficiency.
